patterns.name: Skipping the 'Explain' Step
patterns.what: JeremiahSy occasionally answers the first part of a question but tends to leave the 'explain why' section blank or incomplete.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: JeremiahSy correctly chose the material but missed out on writing the explanation for why it was the better conductor.
He left this multi-part question blank, missing the chance to explain which material reflects light best.
He skipped this question, missing out on explaining how the flower could still grow into a fruit.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see 'explain why' or 'give a reason', always use the **Choice + Evidence + Science Rule** method. Double-check that every question mark on the page has a matching sentence in your answer!

patterns.name: Mixing Up Plant & Human Parts
patterns.what: When comparing flowers and humans, JeremiahSy sometimes mixes up which specific parts perform the same reproductive jobs.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: He chose an option that mismatched the functions of the plant and human reproductive parts.
JeremiahSy had a little trouble matching the male part of the flower to the male part of the human.
He matched the wrong labels when trying to find the female parts that have a similar function.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see diagrams of both a flower and a human, draw a quick line connecting the **male parts (anther to testis)** and the **female parts (ovary to ovary)** before looking at the options.

patterns.name: Juggling Multiple Statements
patterns.what: In questions with several statements to evaluate, JeremiahSy sometimes includes a tricky false statement in his final choice.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: He selected all four statements as true, missing that the unfertilised egg does not develop in the womb.
JeremiahSy picked statements P and Q, overlooking that statement S was the correct description of how the fruit developed.
He missed identifying one of the incorrect statements about heat transfer.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see statements labeled A, B, C, D, treat each one as a separate True/False question. Put a **big tick or cross** next to each letter before looking at the 1, 2, 3, 4 options below.

patterns.name: Tracing Invisible Paths
patterns.what: JeremiahSy sometimes finds it tricky to identify the exact direction that invisible things travel, like which way heat flows or how light bounces.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: He chose an option about the air gaining heat, rather than tracing how the sweat gains heat directly from the body.
JeremiahSy picked arrows that didn't quite show the light bouncing from the door directly into Peter's eyes.
He misjudged how moving the objects would change the path of light to make the shadow shorter.

patterns.strategic_advice: When a question is about heat or light, always draw **arrows on the diagram** to show the path. Remember that heat always flows from **hotter to colder**, and light must bounce from the **object into the eye**.
